stats.description: Cash you can spend right now in the game—on learning items or on investments.
How many different investments you can hold at the same time. Each slot is one type of investment.
How many learning cards you can keep. Same card twice can level up, like merging in other games.
How much volatility and drawdown you can psychologically accept.
Your understanding of investing concepts and disciplined decisions.
Each month this amount gets added to your investing pot—like an automatic piggy bank.
Roughly how much your mix of investments might grow over time. Higher can mean more gain, but nothing is guaranteed in real life.
How wild the ride is—big ups and downs mean higher volatility. Calm and steady means lower.
You aren't betting everything on one thing. Spread out usually means less heartbreak if one investment tanks.
A score that mixes "how much you might earn" with "how bumpy the path is." Higher often means a nicer balance, not just chasing the riskiest option.
Fees and costs that quietly eat part of your returns—like a small leak in a bucket.
How easily you could turn your investments back into cash if you needed money in a hurry.
Part of what you earn that goes to taxes instead of staying in your pocket.
How strongly market drama hits your mood—panic selling or hype buying vs staying steady.
How much money-and-investing stuff your character understands. Learning items bump this up.
How many years you're planning to leave this money invested before you need it for something real.
Out of what you earn, what percent you save instead of spending it all.
How much FOMO, fear, or habit might push you into bad choices instead of thinking twice.
